segments.s555-g17: ستیزه بود is a descriptive past clause: ستیزه (adjective 'quarrelsome') + بود ('was'). مرد را پیش رو is a fronted phrase where مرد + را (definite/object marker) + پیش رو ('in front / in the presence of'); را here emphasizes/definites مرد and links that noun to the preceding predicate. Word order is poetic: adjective+بود then the object/locative phrase giving where/with whom the quality applied.
segments.s555-g18: بماند is a present/subjunctive/past-stative verbal form meaning 'remained' or 'continued to be' and governs نیازش ('his plea/need') as its subject. همه‌ساله نو is an adverbial phrase 'every year (همه‌ساله) new/fresh (نو)', modifying بماند to say the plea kept being fresh annually. The sequence shows poetic inversion: verb first, then its nominal subject and temporal qualifier.
segments.s555-g19: همان here is a demonstrative intensifier ('that very' / 'the same') qualifying رشک ('envy'). The clause reads like a nominal equation: 'That very envy was (بود) the sword (شمشیر) of the ignorant (نادان)'. The copula بود is past/stantive 'was', linking subject and predicate in typical Persian order.
segments.s555-g20: همیشه ('always') is an adverb modifying the clause. برو here appears bound to بخت (forming a poetic unit like 'بروبخت' / 'fortune that goes before'); syntactically the phrase says 'the smiling fortune was ever before him' with بود as the copula. خندان qualifies بخت ('smiling fortune'); the overall structure is adverb + (compound) noun phrase + بود.
segments.s555-g21: دگر هرک ('any other/whoever') begins a conditional/general clause: دگر (else/other) + هرک (whoever). دارد ('has') is present indicative here and takes ز هر کار ننگ ('shame from every action') as its object/compliment; ز is the poetic preposition 'from/because of'. Word order is typical Persian: subject phrase + verb + complement.
segments.s555-g22: بود is the copula 'was/will be' used here to state the consequence: 'the person's life and days were/will be narrow.' زندگانی و روزیش ('life and his days' — i.e. livelihood/daily provision) forms a coordinated subject and تنگ ('narrow/tight') is the predicate adjective. The clause gives the result of the general condition in the previous segment.
segments.s555-g23: در آز باشد is a locative/stative expression: در + آز ('in greed') + باشد (present/stative 'is') — so the clause says 'in greed lies/remains the heart'. دل سفله مرد ('the heart of a base/vile man') is the logical subject in Persian genitive order (دل + سفله مرد). The verb باشد is a poetic/stative copula rather than an imperative.
segments.s555-g24: بر سفلگان ('toward/on the lowly') gives the target group (سفلگان = 'the lowly'). تا توانی ('as far as you are able') is a common adverbial phrase meaning 'to the extent you can'. مگرد is an archaic negative/prohibitive form meaning 'do not go/associate' or 'do not turn (to)'; together the phrase is an admonition: 'Do not (as far as you can) go toward the lowly.'
segments.s555-g41: همه سخته باید که راند سخن is built around the impersonal modal باید + که + verb: باید که راند (one must/it is necessary that one drive away). همه سخته qualifies باید adverbially — 'completely/with severity' — and سخته is an adverb/adjective notion ('harshly' or 'with difficulty'). سخن (object) follows the verb راند (drive away/expel).
segments.s555-g42: که here is the subordinating conjunction linking the purpose/result clause. گفتار نیکو is the noun phrase 'good speech'; نگردد is present subjunctive/optative (negated by initial ن-) of گشت/گردیدن meaning 'become' — 'may not become'; کهن is the predicate adjective 'old/ancient'.
segments.s555-g43: نباید is an impersonal negative modal 'one should not/it must not be'; که introduces the subordinate clause; گویی here functions as 'you say' / 'one says' (a verb form), and به جز نیکوی is a prepositional phrase 'except for the good' (به جز = 'except', نیکوی = 'good/excellent').
segments.s555-g44: وگر is the conditional 'but if / if however'; بد is the adverb/adjective 'bad(ly)'; سراید is a subjunctive/present-form verb 'he composes/sings' (classical Persian uses the bare stem for subjunctive here). نگر introduces the admonition and is used as an imperative/particle meaning 'do not regard/pay attention' (here linking to the following negative verb). نشنوی is the negative subjunctive/present 'you should not hear / do not listen'.
segments.s555-g45: ببیند is a third-person optative/subjunctive form of دیدن ('may he/it see'); the subject phrase دل پادشا ('the king's heart') follows as the logical subject/agent that 'sees'; راز تو ('your secret') is the object. Word order is flexible: verb first (optative), then subject, then object.
segments.s555-g46: همان is a demonstrative 'that/the same' pointing back to the king's heart; بشنود is subjunctive/optative 'may (it) hear'; گوش آواز تو is a noun phrase with گوش 'ear' as the topical/experiencer noun and آواز تو 'your voice' as the object—literally 'may that ear hear your voice.'
segments.s555-g47: چه introduces the question 'what'; the following گفت would normally be the past-tense verb 'said/answered' (what did he say), but the listed senses for گفت are noun senses (conversation) so the best mapping is unavailable here. آن سخن‌گوی is the nominative 'that spokesman/speaker', and پاسخ is 'answer/reply' (object or appositive). نیوش in classical Persian often appears as a name/epithet; the provided senses don't match its use here.
segments.s555-g48: که is the subordinating conjunction 'that' introducing reported speech; دیوار دارد is a simple present construction 'the wall has' (دارد = 'has'); به گفتار گوش is a prepositional/complement phrase 'an ear for speech' (به marks relation/recipient and گفتار 'speech' governs گوش 'ear' as what the wall 'has' for). The whole clause states the reported content.
segments.s555-g9: بکوشیم is a first-person-plural subjunctive/imprecative (‘let us strive’); the verb precedes its complement as is common in Persian poetry. تا here introduces purpose ('so that / in order that'), followed by نیکی آریم where آریم is a present-subjunctive/optative form of آوردن (‘bring/produce’): “let us strive so that we may bring goodness.” The clause ends with و داد, linking a second coordinated object ‘and justice.’
segments.s555-g10: خنک is an adjective (‘refreshing / pleasant’) qualifying داد from the previous segment. آنک is a relative particle here ('which / that'), introducing the relative clause پند پدر کرد یاد, where کرد یاد is a light-verb construction meaning ‘caused to be remembered / recalled’ (پند پدر = 'the father's advice'). The whole relative clause specifies what kind of justice is meant.
segments.s555-g11: چو here = 'when'. یزدان is 'God' and نیکی‌دهش is a compound epithet 'giver of goodness / benefactor'. نیکوی is an adjective ('good, excellent') that links as the thing bestowed; the whole phrase sets the temporal/causal frame: 'when God, the giver of goodness, (gave) good...'.
segments.s555-g12: بما داد is a verb phrase: بما ('to us') + داد ('gave'). The conjunction و links the next noun phrase تاج سر خسروی = 'the crown (تاج) of the head (سر) — i.e. the royal crown' where خسروی qualifies تاج as 'royal/kingly'.
segments.s555-g13: به نیکی کنم is a verbal clause meaning 'I will act kindly / I will do good' (کنم = 1st-person present/subjunctive/intentional). به here marks manner ('by/with / in'), and ویژه ('special') modifies the following noun. انبازتان is an antiquated/obscure noun with an uncertain meaning; it carries the 2nd-person plural possessive suffix -تان ('your'), so the speaker promises special kindness toward whatever this term denotes.
segments.s555-g14: نخواهم که is a negative desiderative 'I will not wish / I do not want that...'; که introduces the subordinate clause. بی من بود is a verbless/nominal clause with بی 'without' + من 'me' + بود 'be/was' meaning 'be without me', and رازتان 'your secret' is the subject/possessed noun: 'I do not want that your secret be without me' (i.e. I don't want to be excluded from your confidences).
segments.s555-g15: بدانید is an imperative/plural polite 'know / understand'. کان کو is an archaic correlating pair introducing a relative/generalizing clause ('that which / whoever'). من ی (من + ی) here marks the relation to the speaker ('to me / of me') and فش بود is 'was an oppression/pressing' (فش = pressure/oppression, بود = was). The overall sense: 'know that whoever was an oppressor of me...'.
segments.s555-g16: بر here has the sense 'toward / to' (i.e. with respect to). مهتران literally 'the great ones / elders / chiefs' (poetic plural), سخت 'severely / greatly' intensifies ناخوش 'displeased / displeasing', and بود is the copula 'was'. So the clause says that such a person was greatly disliked by the chiefs.
segments.s555-g49: همه انجمن is a plural noun phrase meaning 'the whole assembly / everyone of the assembly' (subject). خواندند is 3rd person plural past of خواندن 'they read/uttered', so the verb follows the subject here; آفرین is the uttered acclamation (direct object/quoted word). Word order is typical Persian S-V-(O/quote) in this poetic line.
segments.s555-g50: بران is a prepositional/adverbial form meaning 'for/on/upon that' (i.e., 'for that [man]'). شاه is the head noun 'king' and the following epithets بینادل و پاک‌دین are linked as descriptive titles; Persian often omits an explicit copula, so these epithets attach directly to شاه. پاک‌دین is an epithet 'pure/devout in religion'; بینادل is a rare/uncertain word here and functions as an epithet as well.
segments.s555-g51: پراگنده گشت is a two-word predicate: پراگنده 'scattered/ dispersed' + گشت (past of گشتن) 'became/was turned', so 'became dispersed'. The subject آن بزرگ انجمن ('that great assembly') follows the predicate, a common poetic word order (V‑S).
segments.s555-g52: همه شاد = 'all were glad' (subject + predicate adjective). زان = 'from that / because of that' introduces the cause, and سرو سایه فگن is an attributive phrase 'the cypress (سرو) that casts/has cast shade' where فگن is an archaic verb meaning 'to throw/cast' used here as a past/attributive form. The whole phrase means people rejoiced because of the shade provided by that cypress.
segments.s555-g53: همان رسم = 'that same custom/rite'. The following names 'شاپور شاه اردشیر' stand in apposition/possession: 'the custom of Shapur, (the) Shah Ardashir' — classical Persian frequently places possessor/name after the noun without an explicit ezāfe in epic diction, so this indicates whose custom it is.
segments.s555-g54: همی داشت is a habitual/continuous past construction: همی 'always/still' + داشت (past of داشتن used here as a stative/coprular verb) giving 'was/kept being'. آن شاه is the subject 'that king' and دانش‌پذیر is a predicate adjective/noun 'receptive to knowledge / teachable'. Together: 'that king was always receptive to learning.'
segments.s555-g55: جهانی سراسر is a compound meaning 'the whole world' (literally 'world entirely'); بدو is the dative pronoun 'to him / toward him'; گشت شاد is 'became happy' (گشت = became, شاد = happy). The verb precedes its subject/agent in this poetic order, with بدو marking the beneficiary.
segments.s555-g56: چه نیکو is an exclamatory phrase 'how good' (چه = how), followed by بود 'was' (past copula) and شاه 'the king' as subject. با بخش و داد is an instrumental/qualifying phrase 'in/with generosity (بخش) and justice (داد)', describing the manner of his kingship. The whole is an exclamatory appraisal: 'How good a king he was, in generosity and justice.'
segments.s555-g33: دل شاه is a noun phrase meaning "the king's heart" (دل modified by شاه). کز is a classical fused relative meaning "that/from which" (equivalent to که + از) and introduces the relative clause. مهر دوری گرفت is a past action: مهر "affection" + دوری گرفت literally "took distance/underwent separation" (گرفت is past 3rd sg.), so the whole segment means "the king's heart that became distant from love."
segments.s555-g34: اگر introduces a hypothetical/conditional clause. بازگردد is a 3rd-person present-subjunctive/optative used for a counterfactual or potential return ("should return"/"were to return"). نباشد شگفت is an inversion where نباشد (negative subjunctive of بودن) plus the predicate شگفت means "it would not be a wonder" or "it would not be surprising."
segments.s555-g35: هرانکس که is a classical relative "whoever/whosoever." باشد is a present/subjunctive form of بودن here introducing the state "is." مرا زیردست is an older-style order where مرا (the clitic pronoun expressed as م + را in the segmentation) marks the one to whom subordination applies and زیردست means "under (someone's) control/subject to" — together: "whoever is my subordinate / is under me."
segments.s555-g36: همه is the quantified subject "all / everyone." شادمان is the predicate adjective "joyful/happy." باد here is the optative particle ("may"), so همه شادمان باد = "may all be joyful." و links the coordinated predicate یزدان‌پرست, giving "and God-fearing / pious."
segments.s555-g37: به governs the following noun phrase and here means "by/with / in accordance with." خشنودی is "pleasure/approval," and کردگار جهان is a genitival phrase "the Creator of the world" (کردگار modified by جهان). Together the phrase sets the frame: "by the approval/pleasure of the world's Creator."
segments.s555-g38: خرد is the noun "wisdom." یار باد is an optative construction: یار "companion/ally" + باد (optative "may it be") = "may wisdom be a companion/ally." آشکار و نهان are coordinated adjectives/adverbs "manifest (openly) and hidden," specifying that wisdom should be a companion both publicly and privately.
segments.s555-g39: خردمند‌گر is an archaic/poetic conditional formation: خردمند + گر (گر = "if/should be") giving "if (one is) wise." مردم پارسا is a nominative apposition meaning "a pious person of the people / among the people." Together this is the protasis: "If a wise, pious man of the people..."
segments.s555-g40: چو here means "when" (or "if, when"). جایی is an adverb "somewhere/at some place." سخن راند is an idiomatic verbal phrase: سخن "speech" + راند (perfect/past-stem used here in the sense of 'utter/propel/express' in poetic usage) — literally "uttered/put forth speech." از پادشا uses از with the sense "about/concerning" the noun پادشا (king), so the clause reads "when, at some place, he speaks about the king."
segments.s555-g57: همی + past verb (راند) is a poetic/habitual past construction meaning 'used to/continually did'. The verb راند ('drove/ruled') comes before the affected noun کار at the end; the two prepositional phrases با شرم and با داد ('with modesty' and 'with justice') modify how he acted. Subject is omitted and understood from context ('he' — the king).
segments.s555-g58: چنین 'thus/so' frames the manner; تا is the temporal 'until' introducing the clause that follows. برآمد is a past intransitive 'arose/appeared'. برین is the adjective 'بر' + suffix 'ین' forming 'برین' = 'supreme/exalted', modifying روزگار ('time/age'). Word order places the temporal/result clause after 'تا'.
segments.s555-g59: بگسترد is the past/past-habitual form 'spread' (be- prefix + گسترد). The direct object کافور follows the verb and the prepositional phrase بر جای مشک ('in the place of musk') indicates substitution ('in place of' — جای used as 'instead').
segments.s555-g60: گل و ارغوان ('flowers and crimson') is the nominative/theme that شد ('became/turned into') — شد used intransitively. به here marks the location/setting ('into/in'), پالیز 'orchard/garden' is the place, and خشک is the adjective 'dry'. The clause describes the orchard turning into/being filled with flowers and crimson.
segments.s555-g61: سهی (a poetic epithet meaning 'dark') modifies سرو 'cypress'; او is the third-person pronoun 'his/it/he' and گشت is the past 'became/turned'. همچون introduces a simile 'like/as' and کمان is the object of comparison. The clause literally: 'his dark cypress became like a bow.'
segments.s555-g62: نه آن بود کان is a classical negated copular construction meaning 'it was not that...' (کان = 'as if/that'). شاه را indicates the person involved (the king) and بدگمان is the predicate 'suspicious' — overall: 'it was not that the king was suspicious.'
segments.s555-g63: نبود is the negative past existential 'there was not'. از جهان ('from the world') gives the scope/location. شاد بس روزگار strings together 'happy' (شاد) and بس ('enough/sufficient') modifying روزگار ('days/times') — together: 'sufficient happy times' or 'many/lasting happy days' which are said not to exist.
segments.s555-g64: سرآمد here is a noun 'the end/conclusion' (not 'foremost'), بران is a short adverbial/prepositional element 'upon/for him' (pointing back to the person affected), and دادگر شهریار is the noun phrase 'the just sovereign/king'. Together: 'the end came upon that just king.'
segments.s555-g25: هرانکس که = 'whoever / anyone who' introduces a conditional relative clause. دانش نیابی uses نیابی (subjunctive/negative-present) to mean 'you do not find/attain knowledge' (i.e., 'if you do not find learning in'); برش is the clitic construction بر + ش ('on/to him') that will serve as the object/target for the next clause (so this segment literally: 'whoever you do not find knowledge in, toward him').
segments.s555-g26: مکن is a negative imperative 'do not' (here addressed to the listener). ره‌گذر (a noun) follows elliptically: 'مکن ره‌گذر' = 'do not be a passerby / do not pass him by.' تازید is a present/imperative sense 'rush / hurry' (here: hasten), and بر درش = 'to/upon his door' (بر = on/upon, در = door, ش = his). Together the clause advises: don't pass him by; hurry to his door.
segments.s555-g27: به introduces the beneficiary/description 'for / to a man'. مرد is the head noun 'man / person' followed by a string of postpositive qualities joined by و (خردمند و فرهنگ و رای = 'wise and learned and of counsel'). This is a standard Persian sequence of noun + adjectives/qualities.
segments.s555-g28: بود is the past copula 'was' used here to state a fact. جاودان qualifies the following noun phrase 'eternal'; تخت شاهی = 'the throne of kingship' (تخت = throne, شاهی = kingship). به پای 'at his feet' indicates position/possession (i.e. the throne lay forever at his feet). Word order is poetic but follows the pattern: [subject/predicate] + بود.
segments.s555-g29: دلت is دل + 2nd-person suffix ت = 'your heart.' زنده باشد uses the subjunctive/optative sense of باشد to express a wish ('may ... be alive'). به فرهنگ و هوش indicates the means/qualities ('in/with learning and intelligence').
segments.s555-g30: به بد در جهان = 'toward/into evil in the world' (به marks direction/relatum, بد = evil, در جهان = 'in the world'). تا توانی = 'as long as you can' (تا = 'as long as / until', توانی = 'you are able'). مکوش is a negative imperative 'do not strive / do not attempt' — here advising: avoid evil while you can.
segments.s555-g31: خرد همچو آبست is a simile: خرد ('wisdom') + همچو ('like') + آبست (a poetic contraction equivalent to 'آب است' = 'is water'), so 'wisdom is like water.' و links to دانش زمین = 'and knowledge (is) earth/ground' (earth = foundation/ground). The verb 'is' is implied in the second half through parallelism.
segments.s555-g32: بدان is the imperative 'know' formed from prefix ب- + دان ('to know'). کاین = 'that this' (introducing the content to be known). جدا و آن جدا نیست = 'this separate and that separate is not' — i.e. 'this and that are not separate.' زین here functions poetically as 'from this / of this' (classical contraction), completing the negative statement that the two are not separate.
segments.s555-g1: This is a noun phrase listing two possessions of the king: 'سر' + 'گاه' and 'دیهیم', followed by the owner 'شاه اورمزد'. In classical Persian the possessive relation is expressed by simple juxtaposition rather than an ezafe; here the nouns mean roughly 'throne/head-place' and 'realm/village-land', and 'شاه اورمزد' identifies the owner ('King Ohrmazd'). Word order places the possessed things before the possessor.
segments.s555-g2: بیارایم is a first-person-form of the verb 'to adorn' used here as an optative/intentional ('I will/adorn/I shall make beautiful'). اکنون is the temporal adverb 'now'. چو introduces a simile meaning 'like/as', taking the following noun 'ماه اورمزد' ('the moon of Ohrmazd') as the comparison. Overall this segment is the verb phrase that takes the preceding noun phrase as its object.
segments.s555-g3: این عبارت begins with the prepositional z 'from', followed by 'شاهی' ('kingship'). The short form 'برو' functions here as an archaic contracted phrase (classical 'بر او' = 'upon him/for him'), i.e. a clitic-like element indicating the person affected. 'هیچ تاوان نبود' is the main predication: 'there was no retribution at all.' نبـود is the past existential/negation 'there was not.'
segments.s555-g4: ازان بد که is a contrastive clause-head meaning roughly 'worse than that, that...' (ازان = 'from/than that'; بد = 'bad/worse'; که = conjunction 'that'). عهدش is 'his oath/pledge' with the enclitic -ش 'his'. فراوان نبود is a past negative predication 'was not abundant/many.' The whole clause therefore states the negative point presented as worse than the previous one.
segments.s555-g5: چو is the subordinating conjunction 'when'. بنشست is the past-tense, third-person singular 'sat down'. The subject follows in the usual classical ordering: 'شاه اورمزد بزرگ' = 'the great King Ohrmazd' (name + postpositive adjective 'بزرگ'). The verb-before-subject order for the temporal clause is typical of epic/classical Persian.
segments.s555-g6: به آبشخور آمد is a simple motion clause: preposition به ('toward/into') + place 'آبشخور' ('watering-place') + past verb آمد ('came'). همی is an archaic/emphatic/progressive particle (common in Ferdowsi) that precedes the subject 'میش و گرگ' ('the ewe and the wolf'). Note the verb آمد remains singular despite the coordinated subject — classical Persian often keeps the verb in the singular form.
segments.s555-g7: چنین گفت is 'thus he said' (چنین = 'thus/so'; گفت = 'said' — note the provided sense list lacked the verbal sense, so sense index is left blank). کای is a vocative particle 'O' (or a title 'Kay' in some contexts); نامور is 'renowned/famed'. بخردان is a prepositional/dative-like phrase 'to the wise' or 'among the wise' (ب- + خردان), functioning as part of the address: 'O famed one of the wise.' The verb 'گفت' introduces the following quoted or reported address.
segments.s555-g8: This is a sequence of participial phrases describing the addressee: جهان گشته 'having gone/about the world' uses گشته as a perfect participle; و connects to the next participle; کار دیده 'having seen deeds/affairs' uses دیده (past participle of دیدن). The final item 'ردان' is unclear in the provided sense list (no matching sense), so it is left as unresolved; syntactically it is the noun/descriptor that completes what has been 'seen' or whom the speaker addresses.

sentences.s555-s9: The man was quarrelsome in the presence of others; his plea remained fresh every year.
sentences.s555-s10: That very envy was the ignorant man's sword; his smiling fortune was always before him.
sentences.s555-s11: Whoever else feels shame at every deed will have a narrow life and scant days (i.e., meagre livelihood).
sentences.s555-s12: A base man's heart is given to greed; do not (as far as you can) turn to the lowly.
sentences.s555-s21: One must be very strict in stifling speech, so that fine words do not grow old.
sentences.s555-s22: You should say nothing but what is good; but if he composes badly, do not take heed nor listen.
sentences.s555-s23: May the king's heart perceive your secret; may that same ear hear your voice.
sentences.s555-s24: What did that spokesman answer? That the wall has an ear for speech.
sentences.s555-s5: Let us strive to bring about goodness and refreshing justice — that which our father's counsel recalled.
sentences.s555-s6: When God, the giver of goodness, granted us goodness and the royal crown.
sentences.s555-s7: I will do good, especially for your anbāz (an obscure/old word); I do not wish that your secret be without me.
sentences.s555-s8: Know that whoever oppressed me was most displeasing to the great ones.
sentences.s555-s25: The whole assembly cried “Bravo” for that king — Bīnādel, the devout.
sentences.s555-s26: That great assembly was dispersed; everyone rejoiced at the shade cast by that cypress.
sentences.s555-s27: That same custom of Shapur, King Ardashir, that teachable king observed.
sentences.s555-s28: The whole world became joyful toward him — how good a king he was in generosity and justice.
sentences.s555-s17: If the king's heart, which has grown distant from affection, were to return, it would not be a wonder.
sentences.s555-s18: May everyone who is under me be joyful and God-fearing.
sentences.s555-s19: May wisdom be a companion, manifest and hidden, by the pleasure (approval) of the Creator of the world.
sentences.s555-s20: If a wise, devout man among the people, when at some place he speaks concerning the king...
sentences.s555-s29: He carried out his affairs with modesty and justice in that manner until that exalted time arose.
sentences.s555-s30: He spread camphor in place of musk; the dry orchard became full of flowers and crimson.
sentences.s555-s31: His dark cypress turned like a bow; it was not that the king was suspicious.
sentences.s555-s32: There were no longer sufficient happy days in the world; the end came upon that just sovereign.
sentences.s555-s13: If you do not find knowledge in someone, do not pass him by; hurry to his door.
sentences.s555-s14: A man of wisdom, learning, and counsel had the royal throne forever at his feet.
sentences.s555-s15: May your heart remain alive in learning and intelligence; in this bad world, as long as you can, do not strive (toward evil).
sentences.s555-s16: Wisdom is like water and knowledge like earth; know that 'this' and 'that' are not separate.
sentences.s555-s1: I will now adorn the throne and the realm of King Ohrmazd like the moon of Ohrmazd.
sentences.s555-s2: There was no retribution at all from kingship; worse than that was that his oath was not abundant.
sentences.s555-s3: When the great King Ohrmazd sat down, the ewe and the wolf came to the watering-place.
sentences.s555-s4: Thus he said: 'O famed one among the wise, (you) who have traversed the world and seen affairs, O Radan.'
